does: This paper introduces ROME, a benchmark-construction pipeline that systematically rewrites known unsafe agent trajectories into deceptive variants while preserving risk labels, generating 300 challenge instances from 100 source trajectories across contextual ambiguity, implicit risks, and shortcut decision-making. The authors evaluate how these harder instances degrade safety-judgment performance in LLM-powered tool-using agents, revealing that hidden-risk cases remain challenging even for frontier models. They additionally propose ARISE, an inference-time safety enhancement method using retrieval-guided analogical reasoning with ReAct-style trajectories to improve safety judgment on these harder instances.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on safety evaluation for autonomous LLM agents operating in real environments like the web and operating systems. It responds to limitations in existing safety benchmarks that focus on explicit, straightforward risks and may not capture the deceptive or ambiguous scenarios agents encounter in practice. The paper extends prior work on adversarial evaluation, out-of-distribution robustness, and red-teaming of language models by introducing controlled, systematic methods for generating harder safety instances. It also connects to research on in-context learning and retrieval-augmented inference as mechanisms for improving model behavior without retraining.
enables: This work enables more rigorous evaluation of agent safety by establishing harder, more realistic benchmarks that expose gaps in current models' ability to judge deceptive scenarios. The ROME pipeline and resulting challenge sets can serve as standard evaluation tools for measuring progress in safe agent design. The ARISE approach points toward practical inference-time safety enhancements using analogical reasoning, which could be developed into more sophisticated safety mechanisms for deployed agents. More broadly, the controlled rewriting methodology could inspire similar approaches for stress-testing other aspects of agent behavior, and the emphasis on implicit risks and contextual ambiguity opens research directions into detecting and mitigating subtler forms of unsafe behavior in autonomous systems.
